tooltipEdits.replacement: Points forward to the discourse about to be recited
tooltipEdits.reason: In the opening formula 'evaṁ me sutaṁ', evaṁ is cataphoric: it announces the teaching that follows (as the golden's own core nuance 'Refers to what follows' states). 'Points back to the occasion' asserts the opposite direction and contradicts the core sense.

verdicts.item: Points back to the occasion
verdicts.reason: The original asserts anaphoric (backward) reference, but evaṁ in the opening formula 'evaṁ me sutaṁ' is cataphoric, announcing the discourse to follow — exactly what the golden's own core nuance ('Refers to what follows') states. The tooltip therefore makes a false directional claim in context and directly contradicts the core sense; this is a factual error, not a style preference. Replacement aligns with the core sense.

addSenses.reason: In 'ekaṁ samayaṁ' eka functions as an indefinite article: 'on a certain occasion'. Contextually true and distinct from the bare numeral 'one'.
The adverbial accusative 'ekaṁ samayaṁ' is standardly rendered 'once / at one time'; DPD attests this reading and it is distinct from the numeral sense.

verdicts.item: a; a certain; a single
verdicts.reason: Literally present in b1's acceptedSenses. Context-true: golden tooltip itself says '[Adjective] One, a certain' modifying samayaṁ; 'on a certain occasion' is the mainstream rendering. Distinct content from core 'one' (indefinite vs numeral), so not redundant, and no hedging vector since it is the dominant translation, not a rare homonym.

verdicts.item: once
verdicts.reason: Literally present in b1's acceptedSenses. 'Ekaṁ samayaṁ' is the standard adverbial accusative-of-time idiom rendered 'once/at one time', matching the golden tooltip's '[Accusative of Time]' analysis. A model saying 'once' is correct for this word in this phrase, not vague; content is distinct from 'one' and 'a certain'.

addSenses.sense: name of a town in the Kuru country
addSenses.reason: Contextually true and adds real content beyond the bare 'Place name' gloss: it identifies the referent as a town and locates it in the Kuru country, exactly what the phrase 'kurūnaṁ nigamo' asserts.

addSenses.sense: name of the people of Kuru; Kurus
addSenses.reason: The genitive plural here refers to the Kuru people ('a town of the Kurus'); this sense identifies who the Kurus are (a people/tribe), which the existing purely grammatical glosses ('of the Kurus') do not convey. The alternative homonym 'name of a country' is excluded since the plural here denotes the people, not the country.

verdicts.item: name of a town in the Kuru country
verdicts.reason: Verbatim member of d1's acceptedSenses. Contextually true in MN 10 (the phrase itself says it is a nigama of the Kurus). Adds real referent content (town + Kuru location) beyond the bare 'Place name' core gloss; requires a specific correct answer, so no hedging vector.

verdicts.item: name of the people of Kuru; Kurus
verdicts.reason: Verbatim member of d3's acceptedSenses. Genitive plural here denotes the Kuru people, so contextually true. Adds meaning beyond the grammatical core glosses by identifying the referent as a people and disambiguating against the 'name of a country' homonym (which the curator rightly excluded — that one WOULD be a hedging vector). Any answer matching it already substantially matches 'of the Kurus', so no new vague-credit path opens.

tooltipEdits.replacement: Like the "e-" augment in the Greek aorist (Sanskrit uses "a-")
tooltipEdits.reason: The Greek aorist augment is e- (epsilon, as in élusa), not a-; the a- augment is the Sanskrit/Pāli form. The tooltip teaches a real Indo-European cognate feature but attributes the wrong vowel to Greek.

verdicts.item: Like "a-" in Greek aorist
verdicts.reason: The original makes a false factual claim: the Greek aorist augment is e- (epsilon, e.g. élusa from lúō), not a-. The a- augment is the Sanskrit/Vedic form (á-vocat) that Pāli avoca inherits; both descend from the PIE *é- augment. The replacement preserves the genuine Indo-European cognate point while correcting the vowel attribution, so this clears the falsity bar for tooltip edits.

removeSenses.reason: Wrong homonym in this context: in 'sattānaṁ visuddhiyā' the word is the genitive plural of satta 'being' (for the purification of beings), not the numeral satta 'seven'. A numerical reading is factually wrong here.

verdicts.item: seven types
verdicts.reason: Wrong homonym for this context: in MN 10 'ekāyano ... maggo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā', sattānaṁ is the genitive plural of satta 'being' (for the purification of beings), as the phase's own golden tooltip '[Genitive Plural] Of beings' confirms. The numeral satta 'seven' is a real homonym but no translation or commentary reads this phrase numerically; requiring 'seven types' as a core sense would penalize correct answers and reward dictionary-spraying. The homonym remains properly documented in the tooltip 'Also satta: seven (7)', so no information is lost.

addSenses.sense: attending mindfully; being present with mindfulness
addSenses.reason: DPD's contextual gloss reflecting the sati + upaṭṭhāna analysis (paralleled by Sanskrit smṛtyupasthāna): mindfulness as active attending. Genuinely distinct from the static 'foundations' reading and from the other core senses.

tooltipEdits.replacement: [Compound] pa + √sthā: to stand (also analyzed as sati + upaṭṭhāna, "attending")
tooltipEdits.reason: Wrong prefix: paṭi + √sthā would yield patiṭṭhāna (Skt pratiṣṭhāna), not paṭṭhāna. The attested derivations are pa + ṭhāna ('setting up, foundation') or sati + upaṭṭhāna ('attending', cf. Skt smṛtyupasthāna).

verdicts.item: attending mindfully; being present with mindfulness
verdicts.reason: Redundant: this exact string already appears in p17's acceptedSenses array (twice, verbatim). Adding it again contributes no new discriminative content and only inflates the reference set with duplicate tokens.

verdicts.item: [Compound] paṭi + √sthā: to stand
verdicts.reason: Original is factually false: paṭi + √sthā would give patiṭṭhāna (Skt pratiṣṭhāna), not the geminate paṭṭhāna. Attested derivations are pa + √sthā (Skt prasthāna) or sati + upaṭṭhāna (Skt smṛtyupasthāna, the commentarial/scholarly analysis). The replacement corrects the prefix and notes the upaṭṭhāna analysis accurately.

addSenses.sense: world of experience; (comm) world of aggregates, sense spheres and elements
addSenses.reason: This is the standard commentarial gloss of loka in the satipatthana formula specifically: the 'world' one puts aside covetousness and displeasure regarding is the world of the five aggregates / sense spheres, i.e. the field of experience being contemplated. The aggregates/sense-sphere content is genuinely distinct from the existing generic cores ('in the world', 'regarding the world') and more precise than 'concerning experience'.

verdicts.item: world of experience; (comm) world of aggregates, sense spheres and elements
verdicts.reason: Present verbatim in ae2's acceptedSenses array (not a hallucination). Context-true: the MN 10 commentary glosses loke in this refrain as the five aggregates of clinging (loke ti pancupadanakkhandha), extended to sense spheres/elements — the standard reading for this exact phrase. Not a hedging vector: credit requires the specific, correct commentarial content, not vagueness. Not redundant: shares only 'experience' with the core 'concerning experience'; the aggregates/sense-sphere content is distinct meaning absent from all cores.

addSenses.reason: Contextually true: the dhammas of the fourth satipatthana (hindrances, awakening factors, etc.) are mental states; distinct wording from existing core senses.

addSenses.sense: world of experience; (comm) world of aggregates, sense spheres and elements

verdicts.item: world of experience; (comm) world of aggregates, sense spheres and elements
verdicts.reason: Survives all four rejection tests. (1) It is literally present in ak2's acceptedSenses array in the context file. (2) Context-true: the commentary on the satipatthana formula glosses loke here as the world of the five aggregates / sense spheres and elements, the world of experience, which is precisely the referent in 'vineyya loke abhijjhadomanassam'. (3) Not a hedging vector: it is the contextually correct commentarial reading, not a rare homonym, and it tightens rather than loosens the accepted set relative to the already-present 'universe; cosmos' senses. (4) Not redundant: the aggregates/sense-spheres/elements content appears in no core sense or tooltip; nearest core sense 'concerning experience' lacks that referent, so the add carries genuinely distinct meaning.
